title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series: Ep09 - How to Use SDXL ControlNet Union
description:
In Episode 9 of our ComfyUI tutorial series, we explore ControlNet, focusing on the new Union ControlNet for SDXL. This powerful tool unifies multiple models, such as pose or Canny, into one, making it easier to guide your AI in creating images with specific compositions. Learn how to download, install, and integrate the ControlNet Union ProMax model into your workflow, and discover how to stack multiple ControlNet models for more detailed and controlled results.

In this tutorial, you’ll learn:

- How to download and install the SDXL ControlNet Union ProMax model.
- The basics of ControlNet and how it guides AI to create images with specific structures.
- How to update ComfyUI and explore the new interface options.

Download Control Net Union
File Name: diffusion_pytorch_model_promax.safetensors
https://huggingface.co/xinsir/controlnet-union-sdxl-1.0/tree/main
Place it in ..ComfyUI\models\controlnet folder

Get the custom nodes

Go to Manager - Custom Nodes Manager
Search for venture
transcript: Welcome to Episode 9 of our Comfort UI Tutorial series.  Today, I want to talk about ControlNet, specifically the Union ControlNet for SDXL. However, now we have a unified model that includes all these options in one.  This model contains 12 controls.  You can find more information and examples on the page. ControlNet models are designed to work only with the particular AI model they were made for.  For instance, a ControlNet for SD 1.5 works only with SD 1.5 models. If you run it and nothing happens,  it's because the pre-processor is set to none.  By the way, if you're wondering how,  while I have the runtime displayed on top of the node,  it's due to a previous node I installed in another episode. Let's go with Cany.  Running it will give you a Cany map.  What does this pre-processor do?  Before the image goes through the AI model,  it's analyzed by the pre-processor.  It acts like a filter or scanner  that extracts certain features from the image,  such as edges, outlines, or depth.  It simplifies the input image into something  that AI can easily understand.  Think of the pre-processor as creating a blueprint.  If you're building a house,  you don't start by randomly placing bricks.  You first make a blueprint that shows  where everything will go.  The pre-processor creates this blueprint for the AI.  From here, you can change the type of model you're using.  I will choose the SDXL version,  even though I haven't added the SDXL workflow yet,  just so you can get familiar with the settings.  For resolution, you can choose either 512 or 1024.  Running it at a higher resolution  provides more pixels and better detail.  You can go higher if you need to capture fine details,  but there is a limit.  Going too high will result in an error. For example, if you duplicate the nodes,  you can connect them to get both a depth and cany map  for an image with a building.  Now, let me quickly explain some of the pre-processors I use most often.  Cany detects edges in an image,  think of it like tracing the outline of an object  with a pencil, it highlights the major lines and borders.  It's useful for generating images  where the structure of objects is important  such as detailed line art or architectural designs.  Depth understands the 3D structure of an image.  Imagine looking at a photo  and recognizing what is closer to you  and what is farther away.  It helps create images with a sense of depth,  making it useful for realistic scenes  where perspective matters.  MLSD detects straight lines in an image  such as those in architectural photos.  It's like using a ruler to draw straight lines on a sketch,  highlighting the structure of buildings  or other geometric forms.  It's great for architectural designs.  You do it cityscapes or any scene  where straight lines are prominent.  Normal map analyzes the surface normals,  which are vectors indicating  the direction surfaces are facing.  I'm thinking of it as understanding the texture of a surface,  such as whether it's bumpy, flat, or curved.  It's useful for generating detailed textures  or enhancing the realism of 3D-like images.  Open-posed-to-text human poses  by identifying key points on the body,  like joints.  Imagine drawing a stick figure  to show how a person is standing or moving.
